_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-15 Sexual Assault (Revised 05/23/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-15: Sexual Assault — MDHHS Approved 5/23/23, Reviewed 2023

sections.title: General Management
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-15: Sexual Assault — General Management
points: Follow General Pre-Hospital Care-Treatment Protocol
Victims commonly require psychological support; be thoughtful with speech and movement
Touching may be traumatic: explain what you are doing before procedures or assessment
Treat life-threatening injuries or other emergencies first according to protocol
During transport, allow the patient to choose the preferred attendant when possible

sections.title: Evidence Preservation
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-15: Sexual Assault — Evidence Preservation
points: Maintain evidence and refer to Crime Scene Management-Procedure Protocol
Do not cut through tears or stains; cleanse skin only when necessary for immediate treatment
If clothing is removed, place each item in a paper bag and bring it with the patient when possible
Explain why the patient should not eat, drink, smoke, bathe, change clothing, or go to the bathroom before forensic evaluation; if they must urinate, ask that they not wipe
Do not communicate details of a sexual assault over an open radio channel; use telephone or another secure electronic communication method

sections.title: Strangulation Assessment
points: Strangulation is frequently underrecognized — ask about and assess specifically
Signs and symptoms of strangulation or neck injury are not visible more than 50% of the time
Evaluate for loss of consciousness, inability to recall how they became unconscious, voice change, involuntary urination, or defecation
Visible signs may include neck injury, redness, scratches, rope marks, bruising, thumb-print bruising, and red eyes
Symptoms may include spasms of the neck or throat
Patients may initially appear uninjured — internal injuries and delayed vascular or neurological complications can occur
If strangulation is suspected: obtain IV access (Specialist skill); cardiac monitoring (Specialist skill); transport to emergency department for advanced imaging
Airway compromise may develop — be prepared to manage airway; refer to Airway Management-Procedure Protocol (Specialist skill)

sections.title: Reporting Requirements
points: If the patient desires and/or mandatory reporting is indicated, notify law enforcement if they are not present
Any incident involving a minor or vulnerable adult is a mandatory reporting event
At the patient's request, further assessment and treatment may be delayed for law enforcement arrival only if no life-threatening condition is present
If the patient declines transport, advise them of risks and document per Refusal of Care, Adult and Minor-Procedure Protocol; encourage follow-up at a local specialized treatment center

sections.title: Patient Communication and Support
points: Communicate with compassion — do NOT express disbelief, assign blame, or make judgments
Explain all procedures before performing them; obtain verbal consent when possible
Allow patient to have a support person present if desired and safe
Minimize painful/invasive interventions to those medically necessary — detailed forensic examination is performed at hospital by trained SANE personnel, not in the field
Mental health support: be prepared to manage acute behavioral health crisis — refer to Behavioral Health-Treatment Protocol as needed

deterioration_flags: Active hemorrhage from traumatic injury — hemorrhage control per Bleeding Control (BCON)-Treatment Protocol; preserve evidence when possible
Strangulation with airway compromise — prepare for advanced airway; transport immediately
Strangulation with altered mental status or neurological symptoms — IV access, cardiac monitoring (Specialist skill); expedite transport; delayed vascular injury possible
Acute behavioral health crisis — refer to Behavioral Health-Treatment Protocol; involve law enforcement if scene safety concern
scope_notes: Traumatic injury management, evidence preservation, patient communication, mandatory reporting: all levels
IV/IO access for strangulation with compromise: Specialist skill
Advanced airway for airway compromise: Specialist skill
SANE exam coordination: receiving hospital responsibility — pre-notify early
